studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is assessing a client with epididymitis. The nurse anticipates which findings on physical examination?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Typical signs and symptoms of epididymitis include scrotal pain and edema, which often are accompanied by fever, nausea and vomiting, and chills. Epididymitis most often is caused by infection, although sometimes it can be caused by trauma. The remaining options do not present all of the accurate manifestations.

optionList.content: Tender, indurated prostate gland that is warm to the touch
Tender, indurated prostate gland that is warm to the touch

studentQuestionArray.content: A client complains of fever, perineal pain, urinary urgency, frequency, and dysuria. To assess whether the client's problem is related to bacterial prostatitis, the nurse reviews the results of the prostate examination for which characteristic of this disorder?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
The client with bacterial prostatitis has a swollen and tender prostate gland that is also warm to the touch, firm, and indurated. Systemic symptoms include fever with chills, perineal and low back pain, and signs of urinary tract infection, which often accompany the disorder.

optionList.content: Administering pain medication just before ambulation
Administering pain medication when the client asks for it
Encouraging the use of the incentive spirometer every 8 hours
Assisting the client to splint the incision during respiratory exercise
Administering pain medication just before ambulation
Administering pain medication when the client asks for it
Encouraging the use of the incentive spirometer every 8 hours
Assisting the client to splint the incision during respiratory exercise

studentQuestionArray.content: A client is having difficulty coughing and deep breathing because of pain after a nephrectomy. Which action by the nurse is helpful in promoting optimal respiratory function?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
The client who has had a nephrectomy may have pain with coughing and deep breathing and other respiratory exercises because the location of the incision is so close to the diaphragm. The nurse assists the client by offering opioid analgesics when due, encouraging incentive spirometer use hourly, and assisting the client to splint the incision during coughing. If the client takes pain medication only before ambulation, control of pain may be insufficient, which will not promote optimal respiratory function (pain medication would be offered 30 to 45 minutes before the client ambulates). Laparoscopic nephrectomy can also be performed. Compared to conventional nephrectomy, the laparoscopic approach is less painful and requires no sutures or staples, involves a shorter hospital stay, and has a much faster recovery.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is reviewing the medical record of a client with a diagnosis of pyelonephritis. Which disorder, if noted on the client's record, would the nurse identify as a risk factor for this disorder?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Risk factors associated with pyelonephritis include diabetes mellitus, hypertension, chronic renal calculi, chronic cystitis, structural abnormalities of the urinary tract, the presence of urinary stones, and the presence of an indwelling urinary catheter or frequent catheterization. The conditions noted in the remaining options are not associated risk factors.

optionList.content: Trauma to the bladder or abdomen
Renal cancer in the client's family
Trauma to the bladder or abdomen
Renal cancer in the client's family

studentQuestionArray.content: A client arrives at the emergency department with complaints of low abdominal pain and hematuria. The client is afebrile. The nurse next assesses the client to determine a history of which condition?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Bladder trauma or injury would be considered or suspected in the client with low abdominal pain and hematuria. Glomerulonephritis and pyelonephritis would be accompanied by fever and are thus not applicable to the client described in this question. Renal cancer would not cause pain that is felt in the low abdomen; rather, the pain would be in the flank area.

optionList.content: A paraplegic client on intermittent catheterization
A paraplegic client on intermittent catheterization

studentQuestionArray.content: Which client is most at risk for developing a Candida urinary tract infection (UTI)?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Candida infections, which are fungal infections, develop in persons who are on long-term antibiotic therapy because an alteration of normal flora occurs. These infections also are commonly seen in clients with blood dyscrasias, diabetes mellitus, cancer, or immunosuppression and in those with a drug addiction.

optionList.content: I will stop antibiotic therapy when pain subsides.
I will exercise as much as possible to stimulate circulation.
I need to use warm tub baths and analgesics to increase comfort.
I will keep fluid intake to a minimum to decrease the need to void.
I will stop antibiotic therapy when pain subsides.
I will exercise as much as possible to stimulate circulation.
I need to use warm tub baths and analgesics to increase comfort.
I will keep fluid intake to a minimum to decrease the need to void.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client with prostatitis following kidney infection has received instructions on management of the condition at home and prevention of recurrence. The nurse determines that education was effective if the client makes which statement?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Treatment of prostatitis includes medication with antibiotics, analgesics, and stool softeners. The nurse also teaches the client to rest, increase fluid intake, and use sitz baths or warm tub baths for comfort. Antimicrobial therapy is always continued until the prescription is finished.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse has performed a nutritional assessment on a client with cystitis. The nurse would tell the client to consume which beverage to minimize recurrence of cystitis?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Water helps flush bacteria out of the bladder, and an intake of 6 to 8 glasses per day is encouraged. Caffeine and alcohol can irritate the bladder. Therefore, alcohol- and caffeine-containing beverages such as coffee, tea, and wine are avoided to minimize risk.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client has been diagnosed with pyelonephritis. The nurse interprets that which health problem has placed the client at risk for this disorder?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Pyelonephritis is most commonly caused by entry of bacteria, obstruction, or vesicoureteral reflux. Risk factors associated with pyelonephritis include diabetes mellitus, hypertension, chronic renal calculi, chronic cystitis, overuse of analgesics, structural abnormalities of the urinary tract, presence of urinary stones, and indwelling or frequent urinary catheterization.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is caring for a client with a bladder infection. The nurse plans care, understanding that the primary risk factor for spread of infection in this client is dysfunction of which structure?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
The ureterovesical junction is the point at which the ureters enter the bladder. At this juncture, the ureter runs obliquely for 1.5 to 2 cm through the bladder wall before opening into the bladder. This anatomical pathway prevents reflux of urine back into the ureter and, in essence, acts as a valve to prevent urine from traveling back into the ureter and up to the kidney.

optionList.content: I would try to maintain an acid ash diet.
I would increase my fluid intake to 3 L per day.
I need to take my daily dose of vitamin C to acidify the urine.
I need to avoid alcohol and highly spiced foods but may continue to drink my coffee every day.
I would try to maintain an acid ash diet.
I would increase my fluid intake to 3 L per day.
I need to take my daily dose of vitamin C to acidify the urine.
I need to avoid alcohol and highly spiced foods but may continue to drink my coffee every day.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse has provided instructions regarding home care measures for a client with acute pyelonephritis. Which statement by the client indicates a need for further teaching?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Clients with acute pyelonephritis would be instructed to try to maintain an acid ash diet, which may be of some benefit. Also, they would increase fluid intake to 3 L per day; this helps relieve dysuria and flushes bacteria out of the bladder. However, for clients with chronic pyelonephritis and renal dysfunction, an increase in fluid intake may be contraindicated. Medications such as vitamin C help acidify the urine. Juices such as cranberry, plum, and prune juice will leave an acid ash in the urine. Caffeine, alcohol, chocolate, and highly spiced foods are avoided to prevent potential bladder irritation.

optionList.content: Monitor urine output once per shift.
Measure specific gravity once per shift.
Encourage an excessive intake of oral fluids.
Ensure that the catheter tubing is not kinked.
Monitor urine output once per shift.
Measure specific gravity once per shift.
Encourage an excessive intake of oral fluids.
Ensure that the catheter tubing is not kinked.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client with a bladder injury has had surgical repair of the injured area with placement of a suprapubic catheter. Which is the most appropriate nursing action to prevent complications of this procedure?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
A complication after surgical repair of the bladder is disruption of sutures, caused by tension on them from urine buildup. The nurse prevents this from happening by ensuring that the catheter is able to drain freely. This involves basic catheter care, including keeping the tubing free from kinks, maintaining the tubing at a level below the bladder, and monitoring the flow of urine frequently. Monitoring of urine output every shift is insufficient to detect decreased flow from catheter kinking. Measurement of urine specific gravity and an excessive intake of oral fluids do not prevent complications of bladder surgery.

Contracting the abdominal, gluteal, and perineal muscles

studentQuestionArray.content: A client who has had a prostatectomy has been instructed in perineal exercises to gain control of the urinary sphincter. The nurse determines that the client demonstrates a need for further teaching when the client states to perform which movement as part of these exercises?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis: Rationale:                                                                                                                           The Valsalva maneuver (bearing down) is avoided after prostatectomy because it increases the risk of bleeding in the postoperative period. An acceptable exercise is to tighten the abdominal, gluteal, and perineal muscles as if trying to prevent urination. Another acceptable exercise is to tighten the rectal sphincter while relaxing the abdominal muscles; this prevents the Valsalva maneuver from occurring.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client is admitted to the emergency department after a fall from a horse, and the primary health care provider (PHCP) prescribes insertion of a urinary catheter. While preparing for the procedure, the nurse notes blood at the urinary meatus. The nurse would take which action?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
The presence of blood at the urinary meatus may indicate urethral trauma or disruption. The nurse notifies the PHCP, knowing that the client would not be catheterized until the cause of the bleeding is determined by diagnostic testing. The other options involve performing the catheterization procedure and therefore are incorrect.

optionList.content: Notify the PHCP before performing the catheterization.

While preparing for the procedure, the nurse notes blood at the urinary meatus. The nurse would take which action?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
The presence of blood at the urinary meatus may indicate urethral trauma or disruption. The nurse notifies the PHCP, knowing that the client would not be catheterized until the cause of the bleeding is determined by diagnostic testing. The other options include performing the catheterization procedure and therefore are incorrect.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client has a tentative diagnosis of urethritis. The nurse would assess the client for which manifestation of the disorder?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Urethritis often results from chlamydial infection and is characterized by dysuria, which is accompanied by a clear to mucopurulent discharge. Because this disorder often coexists with gonorrhea, diagnostic tests are done for both and include culture and rapid assays. Hematuria is not associated with urethritis. Proteinuria is associated with kidney dysfunction.

optionList.content: Alter the perineal pH by using a spermicide with a condom.
Keep follow-up appointments for repeat cultures in 4 to 7 days.
Discontinue antibiotics after 3 weeks of uninterrupted administration.
Identify sexual partners for the past 12 months so that they can be treated.
Alter the perineal pH by using a spermicide with a condom.
Keep follow-up appointments for repeat cultures in 4 to 7 days.
Discontinue antibiotics after 3 weeks of uninterrupted administration.
Identify sexual partners for the past 12 months so that they can be treated.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is planning teaching for a client diagnosed with urethritis caused by chlamydial infection. Which information would the nurse plan to include in the teaching session?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Follow-up cultures are typically done in 4 to 7 days to evaluate the effectiveness of the medication. Using a spermicide does not change the perineal pH. The infection can be prevented by the use of latex condoms. Chlamydial infection is treated with antibiotics, which are not discontinued until the prescribed course is completed. All sexual partners during the 30 days before diagnosis would be notified, examined, and treated as necessary.

optionList.content: Maintain a diet low in protein.
Maintain bed rest when edema is severe.
Maintain a diet low in protein.
Maintain bed rest when edema is severe.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is creating a plan of care for a client with a diagnosis of nephrotic syndrome whose glomerular filtration rate (GFR) is normal. Which interventions would the nurse include in the plan of care? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Controlling edema is a critical aspect of therapeutic management of nephrotic syndrome. If the GFR is normal, dietary intake of proteins is needed to restore normal plasma oncotic pressure and thereby decrease edema. Daily measurement of weight and abdominal girth, and careful monitoring of I&O will determine whether weight loss is caused by diuresis or protein loss. Dietary modifications may include salt restriction and fluid restriction and are based on the client's symptoms. Bed rest is prescribed to promote diuresis when edema is severe.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client is diagnosed with epididymitis. The nurse checks the primary health care provider's prescriptions and expects that which measures will be prescribed? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Common interventions used in the treatment of epididymitis include bed rest with bathroom privileges, elevation of the scrotum, ice packs, sitz baths, analgesics, and antibiotics. A heating pad would not be used because direct application of heat would enhance blood flow to the area, thereby increasing the swelling.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse has provided instructions to a client with a urinary tract infection regarding foods and fluids to consume that will acidify the urine. Which fluids would the nurse include in the client's teaching plan that will aid in acidifying the urine? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
Acidification of the urine inhibits multiplication of bacteria. Fluids that acidify the urine include prune, apricot, cranberry, and plum juice. Carbonated drinks would be avoided because they increase urine alkalinity. Two glasses of milk a day can make the urine more alkaline, which could aid in the development of kidney stones.

optionList.content: A dark and smoky appearance of the urine
A dark and smoky appearance of the urine

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is reviewing the urinalysis results for a client with glomerulonephritis. Which findings would the nurse expect to note? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationale:
In the client with glomerulonephritis, characteristic findings in the urinalysis report are gross proteinuria and hematuria. The specific gravity is elevated, and the urine may appear dark and smoky. Positive ketones are not associated with this condition but may indicate a secondary problem.
